q: If a = 2i + j − k and b = i − 2j + 3k, then a · b is:
exp: a · b = (2)(1) + (1)(−2) + (−1)(3) = 2 − 2 − 3 = −3. Dot product: multiply corresponding components and sum. A negative dot product means the angle between them is obtuse (> 90°).

q: The cross product a × b gives a vector that is:
opts: A) Parallel to both a and b
B) Perpendicular to both a and b
D) In the plane of a and b
exp: The cross product (vector product) a × b produces a vector perpendicular to both a and b, with direction given by the right-hand rule. Its magnitude is |a||b|sinθ. If a and b are parallel, a × b = 0 (zero vector).

q: If a · b = |a||b|, then the angle between a and b is:
exp: a · b = |a||b|cosθ. If a · b = |a||b|, then cosθ = 1, so θ = 0°. The vectors are parallel (same direction). When θ = 90°: a · b = 0. When θ = 180°: a · b = −|a||b|.

q: The value of i × j is:
exp: By the right-hand rule: i × j = k, j × k = i, k × i = j (cyclic). Reverse order gives negatives: j × i = −k. Also: i × i = j × j = k × k = 0 (cross product of parallel vectors is zero).

q: The area of a parallelogram with sides a and b is:
exp: Area of parallelogram = |a × b| = |a||b|sinθ, where θ is the angle between a and b. The area of the triangle formed by a and b is |a × b|/2. The cross product magnitude gives the area of the parallelogram.

q: The scalar triple product a · (b × c) equals:
opts: B) Volume of the parallelepiped formed by a, b, c
D) The dot product of a and b times c
exp: |a · (b × c)| = volume of the parallelepiped with sides a, b, c. If the scalar triple product is zero, the vectors are coplanar (zero volume). The scalar triple product can be computed as the determinant of the 3×3 matrix with a, b, c as rows.

q: If a × b = 0 and a ≠ 0, b ≠ 0, then a and b are:
exp: a × b = |a||b|sinθ n̂. If a × b = 0 and |a|, |b| ≠ 0, then sinθ = 0, so θ = 0° or 180°. Both cases mean a and b are parallel (including anti-parallel). Parallel vectors have no cross product (zero area parallelogram).

q: The angle between a = i + j and b = j + k is:
exp: a · b = (1)(0) + (1)(1) + (0)(1) = 1. |a| = √2, |b| = √2. cosθ = 1/(√2·√2) = 1/2. So θ = 60°. The dot product formula gives the angle directly: θ = cos⁻¹(a·b/(|a||b|)).

q: i × (j × k) is equal to:
exp: First: j × k = i. Then: i × i = 0. So i × (j × k) = i × i = 0. This uses the BAC-CAB rule: a × (b × c) = b(a·c) − c(a·b). Here: j(i·k) − k(i·j) = j(0) − k(0) = 0. The vector triple product is NOT always zero though.

q: The work done by force F = 3i + 4j N through displacement d = 2i + j m is:
exp: Work = F · d = (3)(2) + (4)(1) = 6 + 4 = 10 J. Work is the dot product of force and displacement. The dot product gives a scalar (energy). Only the component of force in the direction of displacement does work.

q: If a = 2i + 3j + k and b = i − j + 2k, then a × b is:
The scalar projection of a = 3i + 4j on b = i is:
exp: Scalar projection of a on b = a · b̂ = a · (b/|b|). Here b = i, |b| = 1, so b̂ = i. Projection = (3i+4j)·i = 3. Geometrically, this is the length of the shadow cast by a onto the direction of b.

q: The cross product is anti-commutative, meaning:
exp: The cross product is anti-commutative: a × b = −b × a. Swapping the order reverses the direction (right-hand rule gives opposite direction). This is a fundamental difference from the dot product, which is commutative: a · b = b · a.

q: If [a b c] = 5, then [b c a] =
exp: The scalar triple product is cyclic: [a b c] = [b c a] = [c a b]. All cyclic permutations give the same value. Anti-cyclic permutations give the negative: [a c b] = [b a c] = [c b a] = −5. So [b c a] = 5.

q: The dot product a · b when a ⊥ b is:
exp: a · b = |a||b|cosθ. When a ⊥ b, θ = 90°, cos 90° = 0. So a · b = 0. This is the key criterion for perpendicularity. i · j = 0, j · k = 0, k · i = 0 (basis vectors are mutually perpendicular).

q: The volume of the parallelepiped with edges a = i + j, b = j + k, c = i + k is:
exp: [a b c] = a·(b×c). b×c = (j+k)×(i+k) = j×i + j×k + k×i + k×k = −k + i + j + 0 = i+j−k. a·(i+j−k) = (i+j)·(i+j−k) = 1+1−0 = 2. Volume = |[a b c]| = 2.

q: For what value of λ are vectors a = 2i + λj + k and b = i − 2j + k perpendicular?
The torque (moment) of force F about a point is given by:
exp: Torque τ = r × F, where r is the position vector from the pivot to the point of application of force. Torque is a vector (cross product), perpendicular to both r and F. Its magnitude is |r||F|sinθ, and it measures rotational effect.

q: If |a × b| = |a · b|, then the angle between a and b is:
exp: |a × b| = |a||b|sinθ and |a · b| = |a||b||cosθ|. Setting equal: |sinθ| = |cosθ|, so tanθ = 1 (for acute angle), θ = 45°. The cross and dot product magnitudes are equal when θ = 45°.

q: The dot product i · i is:
exp: i · i = |i||i|cos0° = 1×1×1 = 1. In general, for unit vector â: â · â = 1. Similarly j·j = 1 and k·k = 1. This is because a vector makes a 0° angle with itself, and cos0° = 1.

q: If three vectors a, b, c satisfy [a b c] = 0, then they are:
exp: [a b c] = a·(b×c) = 0 means the parallelepiped volume is zero, i.e., the three vectors are coplanar (lie in the same plane). Coplanar ⟹ linearly dependent. The scalar triple product is the test for coplanarity.

q: The vector projection of a on b is:
exp: Vector projection of a on b = (a · b̂) b̂ = (a · b/|b|) × (b/|b|) = (a·b/|b|²) b. The scalar projection gives the length; the vector projection also gives the direction. |a|cosθ is the scalar projection formula.

q: The magnitude of a × b where |a| = 3, |b| = 4, and θ = 30° is:
exp: |a × b| = |a||b|sinθ = 3 × 4 × sin30° = 12 × 1/2 = 6. Area of parallelogram = 6 sq units. Compare with dot product: a · b = 3×4×cos30° = 12×(√3/2) = 6√3.

q: The condition for three points A, B, C (with position vectors a, b, c) to be collinear is:
exp: Points A, B, C are collinear if vectors AB and AC are parallel: AB × AC = 0. AB = b − a and AC = c − a. If their cross product is zero, they point in the same (or opposite) direction, meaning A, B, C are on the same line.

q: The value of (a + b) · (a − b) is:
exp: (a + b) · (a − b) = a·a − a·b + b·a − b·b = |a|² − |b|². Since dot product is commutative, a·b = b·a cancels. This is the vector analogue of (x+y)(x−y) = x² − y².

q: The cross product is distributive over addition, i.e.,
opts: A) a × (b + c) = a × b × c
B) a × (b + c) = a × b + a × c
D) a × (b + c) = b × a + c × a
exp: The cross product distributes over vector addition: a × (b + c) = a × b + a × c. However, order matters due to anti-commutativity: (b + c) × a = −(a × b + a × c) = b × a + c × a.

q: If a · b = 0 and a × b = 0, then:
opts: C) At least one of a or b is the zero vector
D) a and b are unit vectors
exp: a · b = |a||b|cosθ = 0 and |a × b| = |a||b|sinθ = 0. Both conditions together imply |a||b|(cos²θ + sin²θ) = 0, so |a||b| = 0. Therefore |a| = 0 or |b| = 0 — at least one is the zero vector.

q: a · (b × a) equals:
exp: b × a is perpendicular to a. Taking dot product of a with a vector perpendicular to a gives 0. Formally: a · (b × a) = [a b a]. The scalar triple product with two equal vectors is always zero (determinant with two equal rows = 0).

q: For vectors a = i + 2j + 3k and b = 3i + 2j + k, a · b is:
exp: a · b = (1)(3) + (2)(2) + (3)(1) = 3 + 4 + 3 = 10. The dot product captures how much two vectors 'align' with each other. Note these vectors are symmetric in a specific way — swapping components gives the same dot product.

q: The area of triangle with adjacent sides a = 2i + j and b = i − 2j is:
opts: C) Both A and C are correct
exp: In 2D: |a × b| = |a_x·b_y − a_y·b_x| = |2×(−2) − 1×1| = |−4 − 1| = 5. Area of parallelogram = 5. Area of triangle = 5/2 = 2.5. Both A (5/2) and C (2.5) are the same value, so D (both A and C) is correct.

q: The BAC–CAB rule states that a × (b × c) =
exp: The vector triple product identity (BAC–CAB rule): a × (b × c) = b(a·c) − c(a·b). Mnemonic: the middle vector times (outer·last) minus last vector times (outer·middle). This is a key identity for simplifying triple vector products.

q: If the dot product of two unit vectors is 1/2, the angle between them is:
exp: â · b̂ = |â||b̂|cosθ = 1×1×cosθ = cosθ = 1/2. So θ = cos⁻¹(1/2) = 60°. For unit vectors, the dot product directly gives cosθ. Common values: cos60°=1/2, cos45°=1/√2, cos30°=√3/2.

q: Which of the following equals zero always for any non-zero vector a?
exp: a × a = |a|²sin0° n̂ = 0 (the angle between a and itself is 0°, sin0° = 0). Also the determinant with two identical rows is 0. a · a = |a|² ≠ 0, a + a = 2a ≠ 0, 2a ≠ 0 for non-zero a.

q: The scalar triple product [a b c] is equal to the determinant with rows:
opts: A) Unit vectors a, b, c
C) Components of a, b, c as rows
D) Magnitudes of a, b, c
exp: [a b c] = a·(b×c) = |a₁ a₂ a₃; b₁ b₂ b₃; c₁ c₂ c₃| — the 3×3 determinant with components of a, b, c as rows. This is the computational shortcut. The absolute value gives the volume of the parallelepiped.

q: If a = 2i − j + 2k, the value of a · a is:
exp: a · a = |a|² = 2² + (−1)² + 2² = 4 + 1 + 4 = 9. In general, a · a = |a|². This is equivalent to the squared magnitude. Note: |a| = √9 = 3, so |a|² = 9.

q: The force F = 2i + j − k acts through point A(1,2,3). The moment about the origin O is r × F where r = OA. The moment is:
The condition for a · b = a · c to imply b = c is:
opts: A) True if a, b, c are unit vectors
B) It does NOT always imply b = c; additional condition a ≠ 0 and a·(b−c) = 0 gives a ⊥ (b−c)
exp: a · b = a · c means a · (b−c) = 0, which means a ⊥ (b−c). This does NOT mean b = c; b−c could be any vector perpendicular to a. This is a critical distinction: cancellation in dot products doesn't work like in scalar algebra.
